verses.text: Ahaz does evil like the kings of Israel, making molten images for Baalim and even burning his own children in the fire of the heathen. He sinks to pagan cruelty. A heart that abandons God will not stop at idols but descends to horrors that natural love forbids.

Ahaz does evil like the kings of Israel, making molten images for Baalim and even burning his own children in the fire of the heathen. He sinks to pagan cruelty. A heart that abandons God will not stop at idols but descends to horrors that natural love forbids.

God delivers Ahaz to Syria and to Israel, who inflict a great slaughter, killing even his son and chief officers. The idolater finds no shelter in his idols. When a ruler forsakes God, the defences he trusted crumble and judgment comes from every side.

Israel carries off two hundred thousand of Judah, but the prophet Oded warns them to return the captives, asking, 'are there not with you... sins against the LORD?' Conquerors are not innocent. God rebukes cruelty to a fallen brother and bids the strong remember their own guilt.

Israel carries off two hundred thousand of Judah, but the prophet Oded warns them to return the captives, asking, 'are there not with you... sins against the LORD?' Conquerors are not innocent. God rebukes cruelty to a fallen brother and bids the strong remember their own guilt.

Certain leaders of Ephraim heed the word, clothe and feed the captives, set the feeble on donkeys, and carry them home to Jericho. Mercy answers the prophet's call. Even amid national sin, tender hearts can shine, showing kindness to the stranger and the weak.

Ahaz appeals to Assyria for help while Edom and the Philistines invade, 'for the LORD brought Judah low because of Ahaz.' He runs to man instead of God. The king's faithlessness drags a whole nation down, and worldly allies cannot lift what God has cast down.

Ahaz appeals to Assyria for help while Edom and the Philistines invade, 'for the LORD brought Judah low because of Ahaz.' He runs to man instead of God. The king's faithlessness drags a whole nation down, and worldly allies cannot lift what God has cast down.

The king of Assyria comes but distresses Ahaz instead of helping, though Ahaz stripped the temple to buy his aid. The bribe buys only trouble. Help sought against God's will turns to harm, and what we sacrifice to win it is simply lost.

The king of Assyria comes but distresses Ahaz instead of helping, though Ahaz stripped the temple to buy his aid. The bribe buys only trouble. Help sought against God's will turns to harm, and what we sacrifice to win it is simply lost.

In his distress Ahaz trespasses still more, sacrificing to the gods of Damascus 'which smote him,' hoping they will help. Affliction hardens rather than humbles him. The unrepentant heart, instead of turning to God under the rod, sins the more and courts deeper ruin.

In his distress Ahaz trespasses still more, sacrificing to the gods of Damascus 'which smote him,' hoping they will help. Affliction hardens rather than humbles him. The unrepentant heart, instead of turning to God under the rod, sins the more and courts deeper ruin.

Ahaz shuts up the temple doors, sets up altars in every corner of Jerusalem, and makes high places in every city of Judah. He floods the land with idolatry. A leader bent on sin spreads it everywhere, dragging his people into the darkness he has chosen.

Ahaz shuts up the temple doors, sets up altars in every corner of Jerusalem, and makes high places in every city of Judah. He floods the land with idolatry. A leader bent on sin spreads it everywhere, dragging his people into the darkness he has chosen.

Ahaz dies and is buried in the city but not in the tombs of the kings of Israel. Even his burial bears the mark of disgrace. A reign spent fighting God ends without the honour reserved for those who served Him.
